text: Hello friends, I am Dr. Rahul Mahadhar, today going to present laparoscopic nascent fundoplication step by step for hiatal hernia. Forty eight year old female patient presented with the volume reflux, upper GI endoscopy revealed hiatal hernia with grade one GRD. There were no comorbidities in this patient and her BMI was thirty two point five. Manometry study in her revealed hypotensive alias with hiatal hernia and there were normal mobility of the esophagus. So plan in this patient is laparoscopic nascent fundoplication. The patient and port position is as shown in the figure. The surgeon is standing in between the leg, two monitors at two shoulder levels of the patient, anesthesia trolley at the head end. and assistant standing on the left side of the patient.

The surgeon uses a Nathanson's retractor to lift the left lobe of the liver, revealing a large hiatal hernia with the stomach protruding into the chest cavity. A 5mm port is placed at the anterior axillary line below the left costal margin. Using a harmonic scalpel, the surgeon performs the past flaccida approach, cutting the peritoneum over the right crus with small bites while achieving hemostasis.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to make a small incision in the peritoneal lining over the right crus, entering a plane to preserve the lining. The pleura is visible, and care is taken not to injure it during dissection. The posterior vagus nerve is identified, and traction is applied to reveal lateral attachments and the peritoneum over the left crus. The anterior vagus is also visible, and the lower esophagus is separated anteriorly while avoiding injury to the vagus nerve. Small vessels are cut with the harmonic scalpel to prevent bleeding. The assistant lifts the OG junction to expose the posterior vagus, and a posterior window is created using a left-hand instrument. Umbilical tape is placed around the OG junction for maneuverability, and dissection is carried out trans-hiatally, separating the posterior vagus from the aorta while avoiding pleural injury.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform small, precise dissections around the left crus, carefully maintaining the peritoneal reflection to facilitate future pleural suturing. The aorta is visible posteriorly, and the pericardium is located above the dissection plane. The surgeon mobilizes the esophagus, measuring its intra-abdominal length with an umbilical tape marked in one-centimeter increments, achieving a mobilization of approximately six centimeters with traction.

The surgeon begins dissecting the short gastric vessels at the lower end of the spleen along the greater curvature of the stomach using a ligature device. The assistant holds the cut end to facilitate the dissection, ensuring the blood supply along the greater curvature is maintained to prevent ischemic injury. Care is taken to avoid bleeding, keeping the surgical field clear.

The surgeon carefully dissects the short gastric vessels near the spleen using ligature and harmonic scalpel, ensuring minimal bleeding. Adhesions are addressed, and the dissection endpoint reveals both crura and the upper border of the pancreas. The fundus wrap is passed through the posterior window, performing the shoeshine maneuver for a floppy Nissen fundoplication. Crural approximation is done using 2-0 sutures, with careful attention to needle entry and exit points to avoid injuring the aorta or causing bleeding from the caudate lobe of the liver.

The surgeon performs coronal suturing, ensuring the approximation is not too tight. The assistant holds the umbilical tape around the lower esophagus as the surgeon prepares for a Nissen fundoplication. Adhesions between the pancreas and the posterior wall of the stomach are identified and carefully dissected to free the posterior part of the stomach.

The surgeon uses a ligature to release dense adhesions on the posterior aspect of the stomach, ensuring it is free for a floppy Nissen fundoplication. The splenic artery is visible and carefully preserved during the dissection to prevent injury. The assistant provides traction around the OG junction, facilitating the creation of a 360-degree wrap.

The surgeon performs a 360-degree wrap, creating a floppy Nissen fundoplication. Sutures are placed laterally, starting from the stomach, then through the esophagus, and back to the stomach, securing the wrap just above the esophagogastric junction.

The surgeon places the third suture at the level of the esophagogastric junction, securing the wrap to the right crura to prevent migration into the chest. The second suture is positioned posterior to the wrap, fixing it to both crura. The procedure is nearly complete, with suction performed and the Ryles tube inserted. The umbilical tape is removed, and the Nathanson's retractor is taken out under visualization, confirming no bleeding in the surgical field.
